# Development Log — India Crude Supply Chain Digital Twin

This is a complete, chronological record of everything built in this project, written for
auditing — so you can find problems, not so it reads well. Every number, file path, and
claim below was checked against the actual files at the time of writing. Where something is
an assumption rather than a fact, it says so explicitly.

**The one-line answer to "where is the extracted data":** `data/network.json`, generated by
`build_network.py` from constants documented in that same script. The provenance of every
number in it — which PPAC/DGCI&S document, which table, which page — is in `DATA_SOURCES.md`.
Full detail in Section 2 below.

---

## 1. Project structure — every file and what it's for

```
ps2-digital-twin/
  app.py                    966 lines — the Streamlit app (everything the user sees)
  build_network.py          417 lines — generates data/network.json from hardcoded, sourced constants
  scenario_engine.py        185 lines — disruption simulation (Layer 2), pure Python, no solver
  optimizer.py              555 lines — the two-stage stochastic LP + CVaR (Layer 3), Pyomo + HiGHS
  signal_extractor.py       308 lines — Layer 1: headline/manual -> structured signal -> scenario
  briefing_generator.py     149 lines — optimizer output -> executive briefing text
  llm.py                     82 lines — pluggable LLM call, real API or deterministic fallback

  data/
    network.json                                          — THE EXTRACTED DATA (see Section 2)
    1778223525_Indias_Oil_Gas_Ready_Reckoner_for_FY_2025-26_version-1.pdf   — user-provided source PDF
    1784287517_Snapshot_of_India_Oil_and_Gas_June_2026_A5.pdf              — user-provided source PDF
    1733897453_RR_2024-25_H1_Final_WebUpload.xlsx                          — user-provided source Excel
    1782821165_ICB Notification_30_06_2026.pdf                             — user-provided source PDF
    dgcis_freeuser_1784552528488_1.xls                                    — user-provided source Excel

  tests/
    test_data_integrity.py     12 tests — is network.json structurally valid
    test_scenario_engine.py    14 tests — disruption-simulation correctness
    test_optimizer.py          15 tests — LP constraint satisfaction, regression tests for 2 known bugs
    test_signal_extractor.py   36 tests — signal extraction + manual override, all boundary conditions
    test_briefing_generator.py  5 tests — briefing edge cases
    test_app_integration.py    13 tests — Streamlit AppTest stress paths
    (95 tests total, all passing as of the last full run)

  SPEC.md                — one-page model spec (decision vars, constraints, objective), written Day 2
  DATA_SOURCES.md         — exactly which PPAC/DGCI&S document/table backs which number
  KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md    — 10 disclosed limitations + the real-world validation writeup
  TESTING.md              — what the test suite covers and, explicitly, what it does NOT cover
  requirements.txt        — runtime dependencies
  requirements-dev.txt    — + pytest
```

---

## 2. The extracted data — where it is and exactly what's in it

**File: `data/network.json`**, regenerated by running `python3 build_network.py`.
As of the last build: **47 nodes, 44 edges.**

Node breakdown: 16 refineries, 13 suppliers (9 import-origin + 4 domestic-production regions),
4 corridors (chokepoints), 11 ports, 3 SPR sites.
Edge breakdown: 21 shipping_route, 16 port_to_refinery, 4 domestic_pipeline, 3 spr_link.

`network.json` also carries a `meta` block with three parts, and this is the part that
actually documents provenance — read it directly (`network["meta"]`) or via
`DATA_SOURCES.md`, which is the human-readable version of the same thing:

- `meta.verified_from_provided_documents` — 24 exact figures pulled from the five user-provided
  documents, e.g. `crude_imports_kbpd_fy2025_26: 4936.2`, `india_basket_crude_price_usd_bbl_fy2025_26_avg: 70.99`,
  `bbl_per_tonne_conversion_factor: 7.33`. Every one of these 24 values is traceable to a specific
  table in a specific source document — see `DATA_SOURCES.md` for the table/page citations.
- `meta.assumptions` — 10 written statements of exactly where a modeling judgment call was made
  instead of a sourced figure (e.g. regional split of domestic production, chokepoint_exposure
  fractions, the ~165 kbpd residual baseline gap — revised down from an earlier ~300 kbpd
  estimate; see the post-audit fixes section below). Read these directly; they are the single
  fastest way to find where this model could be wrong.
- `meta.sources` — the 8 source documents/datasets themselves, named exactly.

### What was extracted from which document

| Document (in `data/`) | What was pulled from it |
|---|---|
| `Indias_Oil_Gas_Ready_Reckoner_for_FY_2025-26...pdf` | All-India refining capacity (256.816 MMTPA as of 01.04.2024), FY2025-26 highlights (258.1 MMTPA nameplate, 22 refineries, 34.3% private share, 272.1 MMT processed) |
| `Snapshot_of_India_Oil_and_Gas_June_2026_A5.pdf` | Crude imports (245.8 MMT FY2025-26), indigenous production (28.0 MMT FY2025-26), India Basket price ($70.99/bbl FY2025-26 avg, $83.22/bbl Jun 2026) |
| `RR_2024-25_H1_Final_WebUpload.xlsx` | Refinery-level capacities (Table 4.1), crude processing mix (Table 4.2/4.3), named pipeline lengths/capacities (Table 5.2: Salaya-Mathura, Paradip-Haldia-Barauni, Mundra-Panipat, Mundra-Bathinda, Vadinar-Bina, Mangla-Bhogat, Duliajan-Digboi-Bongaigaon-Barauni), the 7.33 bbl/tonne conversion factor (Table 8.24 footnote) |
| `ICB Notification_30_06_2026.pdf` | Indian Crude Basket pricing ratio for 15-31 July 2026 (79.40% Dated Brent / 20.60% Dubai-Oman) |
| `dgcis_freeuser_1784552528488_1.xls` | Exact calendar-2025 country-wise crude import quantities/values for 34 countries — this is what replaced an earlier estimated supplier-share breakdown with real DGCI&S figures: Russia 32.66%, Iraq 18.75%, Saudi Arabia 13.23%, UAE 10.75%, USA 7.37%, Kuwait 3.12%, Nigeria 2.94%, Angola 2.10%, Other (26 countries pooled) 9.08% |

**Not in the user-provided documents, sourced separately (flagged in `meta.sources` as
"secondary"):** SPR inventory (Vizag 1.33 MMT, Mangalore 1.5 MMT, Padur 2.5 MMT — from
PIB/Wikipedia, since SPR sits outside PPAC's Ready Reckoner scope). This is the one major
figure in the model that could NOT be cross-checked against an authoritative document, and
it is called out explicitly in `KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md`, item 1.

### Two independent cross-checks that were run on the DGCI&S file

Before trusting `dgcis_freeuser_1784552528488_1.xls` as accurate: dividing its total
value by quantity gives an implied price of $71.18/bbl for calendar 2025, within $0.19 of
PPAC's own $70.99/bbl FY2025-26 figure. Its top-5-country combined share (82.76%) is within
0.19 points of a separately-fetched DGCI&S report's own figure for FY2024-25 (82.57%). Two
different DGCI&S data pulls, two different periods, consistent answers — this is why the file
was trusted rather than taken purely on faith.

---

## 3. Chronological build history

### Day 1 (prior to this session's summarized history): decisions + data + skeleton
- Wrote `SPEC.md`: decision variables (barrels per supplier×route×refinery, SPR drawdown,
  spot purchases), constraints (grade compatibility, port capacity, transit time, SPR limits),
  objective (cost + CVaR of shortfall).
- Built the first version of `data/network.json` with initial estimated (not yet PPAC-verified)
  figures.
- Built the Streamlit skeleton with a pydeck map rendering the network.

### Data verification pass (still early)
- User provided 4 PDF/Excel documents plus a DGCI&S export. All figures in `network.json`
  were reconciled against them; the refinery list grew from 12 to 16; supplier country shares
  went from estimated to exact (the DGCI&S cross-check above); named real pipelines replaced
  invented port-to-refinery capacities. Documented in `DATA_SOURCES.md`.
- SPR primary sourcing was investigated and found not independently verifiable from the
  provided documents (SPR is a separate MoPNG program). User explicitly decided to accept
  this gap and move on to building the system rather than continue chasing it.

### Day 2: Scenario Engine + Optimizer

**`scenario_engine.py`** — 4 named scenarios: `baseline` (no disruption, probability 0.55),
`hormuz_50` (50% capacity loss at Hormuz, 0.25), `hormuz_100` (full closure, 0.05),
`redsea_suspend` (80% suspension at Red Sea/Suez, 0.15). `degraded_capacity()` reduces a
shipping edge's capacity by `severity * chokepoint_exposure`, deliberately NOT auto-rerouting
via any `alt_corridor` field on the same edge — recovery has to come from the optimizer
reallocating to different edges/suppliers, which is the actual point of the exercise.

**`optimizer.py`** — Two-stage stochastic linear program (continuous variables only, no
integers) with an optional CVaR risk-averse term, solved with Pyomo + HiGHS.
- First-stage: `contract[e]` per shipping edge — committed once, before the disruption is known.
- Second-stage (per scenario): `shipping_flow`, `paid_volume`, `flow` (port-to-refinery/domestic),
  `spr_draw`, `spot`, `shortfall`.
- Hand-verified on a 2-supplier, 2-refinery toy case before scaling to the full network.

**Bug found and fixed — CVaR had zero effect.** The first working version made every
variable scenario-indexed with no shared first-stage decision, so risk-neutral and
risk-averse solutions were byte-identical (confirmed by explicitly testing whether λ changed
the solution — it didn't). Root cause: contracting supplier capacity was free unless actually
delivered, so nothing was at stake for CVaR to trade off. **Fix:** made `contract[e]` genuinely
first-stage and added `TAKE_OR_PAY_FRACTION = 0.7` — you pay for ≥70% of what you contracted
even if the scenario can't deliver it, mirroring real term-contract economics. This is the
single most load-bearing fix in the whole model; it's documented in `optimizer.py`'s comments
and in `KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md` item 10 as intentional Q&A material, not something to hide.

**Cost assumptions (all explicit, in `optimizer.py`):** India Basket $70.99/bbl anchor,
per-supplier price differentials (documented judgment calls, e.g. Russia -$8/bbl for the Urals
discount), `SHORTFALL_PENALTY_USD_BBL = 150.0` (empirically found necessary — testing lower
values of 60/40/30/20/12 all caused the model to shortfall 100% of demand, because "not
delivering" became cheaper than "delivering"), `SPOT_CAP_FRACTION_OF_DEMAND = 0.20`.

### Bug reports from the user, all fixed and verified this session

1. **`st.pydeck_chart(deck, width="stretch")` → `TypeError`.** Caused by a global
   `use_container_width=True` → `width="stretch"` find-and-replace that broke specifically on
   the user's installed Streamlit version for `pydeck_chart`. Fixed by reverting to
   `use_container_width=True` everywhere.
2. **Unconnected/disconnected corridor nodes on the map.** Corridor markers were never an
   edge source or target by design, so they looked like floating dots. Fixed by routing
   shipping-route edges THROUGH their `via_corridor` waypoint using a pydeck `PathLayer`
   instead of a direct-line `ArcLayer`.
3. **Nodes in the ocean.** `sup_other`'s placeholder coordinate was `(-5.0, 60.0)` — literally
   open ocean, chosen as a lazy default and never fixed. Moved to Singapore `(1.29, 103.85)`,
   a defensible representative point for the pooled "Other" bucket.
4. **Edges didn't show real routes, couldn't see disruptions.** Fixed via the same
   waypoint-routing change plus moving the scenario picker above the map and adding red
   highlighting for edges/nodes on the active disrupted corridor.
5. **`pyomo.common.errors.ApplicationError: Solver ... is not available (NotFound)`.**
   `appsi_highs` wasn't registering in the user's conda environment. Fixed with
   `_get_available_solver()`, which tries `["appsi_highs", "highs"]` in order and raises an
   actionable error message (naming the exact `pip install` command) if neither works.

### "Triage" pass — honest-caveats review, per explicit user direction

User's own words, paraphrased from the transcript: reframe invented numbers as visible,
testable assumptions rather than removing them; keep the CVaR bug as a documented
technical-excellence story; fix the baseline supply gap and the "MIP" mislabeling (both
described by the user as cheap fixes); write down what's consciously NOT being fixed.

- **Assumptions & Robustness panel** added to `app.py`: four sliders (shortfall penalty,
  spot premium, take-or-pay fraction, P(Hormuz full closure)) plus a "Run robustness check"
  button comparing the base assumptions against whatever the sliders are set to. Tested at
  +50% on all four simultaneously — the honest result was that Iraq and UAE swap in the
  top-2 contracted-supplier ranking at that extreme. This was reported as-is, not smoothed
  over into a false "always stable" claim.
- **Baseline supply gap (~165 kbpd as of the post-audit fixes; originally reported as ~297 kbpd,
  see below) reported net of baseline.** `scenario_engine.summarize()`
  now returns `incremental_gap_kbpd = max(0, this_scenario_gap - baseline_gap)` alongside the
  raw number, so every disruption scenario's headline metric reflects what the disruption
  itself caused, not a topology-completeness artifact that exists even with zero disruption.
  Same netting applied to the optimizer's shortfall reporting
  (`scenario_incremental_shortfall_kbpd`).
- **"Two-stage stochastic MIP" → "two-stage stochastic LP."** The model has never had any
  integer/binary variables; the docstring was simply wrong. Fixed, with an explicit one-line
  defense: staying continuous keeps solve times interactive for a live demo, and nothing in
  the actual decisions (contract sizing, routing) requires integrality.
- **`KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md` written**, covering: SPR sourcing, FY/calendar-year mismatch in
  supplier shares, regional production-split approximation, single-period (not multi-day) SPR
  modeling, the cut fairness objective, the "Other" bucket's approximate chokepoint routing,
  the missing freight/insurance disruption-surcharge mechanism, and the CVaR/take-or-pay bug
  story.

### Real-world validation research

Researched the actual Red Sea/Houthi crisis (Nov 2023-present) as the closest real analog to
`redsea_suspend`, since no real Hormuz full closure existed at the time this research was
done. Key figures found (EIA, Feb 2024): Persian Gulf → Rotterdam via Suez is 19 days, via
Cape of Good Hope ~35 days (+84%); crude flow through Bab-el-Mandeb fell ~18% in December
2023; tanker rates on affected routes rose ~20% in a month, with reports of 35-40% at the
2024 peak; Brent spot stayed roughly flat over the same window (the crisis raised freight
costs, not the commodity price).

**Finding, not designed in:** cross-checking this data against the model's own numbers
revealed that `alt_corridor`/`alt_transit_days` fields exist on every Hormuz/Red-Sea edge in
`network.json` but are **never read** by either `scenario_engine.py` or `optimizer.py` — the
model has no mechanism at all for a reroute's longer transit time or higher freight cost
during a live disruption. This is now documented in `KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md` item 6/7 as a
genuine, disclosed gap between what the data model records and what the code actually uses.

**Mid-session discovery: this stopped being hypothetical.** A search turned up a real, still
-ongoing **2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis** (Wikipedia: US/Israel airstrikes on Iran since 28
Feb 2026, Iran mining and closing the strait, Brent peaking near $126/bbl — the largest
monthly oil-price increase on record — and Indian seafarers among the casualties on the
tankers *Skylight* and *MKD VYOM*). User was asked explicitly how central to make this and
chose to make it the spine of the Day 3 headline set (see below) rather than treat it as one
data point among several, or exclude it.

### Map/geography fixes (later session)

- **`sup_other`'s three Red-Sea-tagged edges were geographically backwards.** Routing a
  Singapore-origin barrel via Bab-el-Mandeb (west of India) to reach India's west coast made
  no sense — Malacca (east of India) is the correct corridor, which its other two edges
  already used correctly. Retagged in `build_network.py`, `network.json` regenerated.
- **Russia's Suez-routed edges drew a straight line across Europe/Africa.** Added Gibraltar
  and Port Said as extra path-rendering-only waypoints (not part of the disruption model
  itself) so the route visibly sails around Europe instead of cutting through land.
- **Edge width was a flat constant regardless of actual capacity.** Changed to scale with
  `capacity_kbpd` on the main map, and with actual solved flow on the Disruption Timeline.
- **Built the Disruption Timeline**: a day slider that interpolates a shipping-route network
  between three real states — day 0 (normal flow), day 1 (instant shock, capacity capped, no
  reallocation), and day N (the optimizer's fully re-contracted plan) — with routes still
  below normal colored red and routes absorbing more than normal colored gold. Explicitly
  disclosed as a dramatized interpolation between two real solved LP states, not a genuine
  multi-day re-solve (the optimizer itself is still single-period).
- **Found and fixed a real Streamlit state bug** while building this: the entire optimizer
  -results section (including the new Day slider) was gated on a raw `st.button()` return
  value, which Streamlit resets to `False` on ANY other widget interaction — so moving the Day
  slider was making the whole results section disappear. Fixed by persisting results in
  `st.session_state` instead of the raw click boolean.

### Day 3: Signal → Scenario → Plan integration

- **`llm.py`**: a single `call_llm()`/`call_llm_json()` interface. Uses the real Anthropic
  API if `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` is set (and the `anthropic` package is installed); returns
  `None` on any failure (no key, network error, malformed response) so callers always have an
  explicit fallback path rather than a crash.
- **`signal_extractor.py`**: 7 pre-cached, dated, real headlines (6 from the 2026 Hormuz
  crisis: strikes on 2026-02-28, tanker attacks 2026-03-01, closure confirmed 2026-03-02,
  Brent >$100 on 2026-03-08, full closure declared 2026-03-27, US naval blockade 2026-04-13;
  1 real Red Sea precedent from 2024-01-16). `extract_signal()` tries the real LLM first, then
  falls back to a deterministic keyword extractor. `nearest_scenario()` maps a signal onto the
  closest of the 4 scenarios.
- **`briefing_generator.py`**: optimizer output → one-page executive note. Real LLM if
  configured (given only pre-computed numbers, instructed not to invent any), else a
  deterministic Markdown template using the exact same numbers.
- **Money-slide chart**: risk-neutral vs. risk-averse, expected cost and worst-case-scenario
  cost side by side (matplotlib). Deliberately does NOT force the plan's illustrative
  "cuts worst-case shortfall 60%" framing, since this model's actual finding is more precise:
  physical shortfall is capacity-bound and doesn't move; its COST does.
- **End-to-end wiring**: one click ("Extract signal & build plan") runs extraction, scenario
  selection, both optimizer solves, and briefing generation, and reports the total elapsed
  time — "Headline → executable plan in 0.16 seconds" in testing, directly answering the
  plan's target demo line.
- A bug was found in the briefing template while building this: a leftover string-continuation
  artifact was leaking literal `"` characters into the middle of sentences. Fixed by using
  proper line-continuation backslashes in the f-string.

### First real test pass (this session, explicit user request: "how do we know this doesn't break")

Built 81 pytest tests across 5 files (later grown to 95 — see below), specifically trying to
break things rather than confirm happy paths.

**One real bug found and fixed:** `optimizer.run_full_network(scenario_keys=[])` silently
substituted the full 4-scenario default instead of erroring or running zero scenarios,
because the code used `scenario_keys or list(SCENARIOS.keys())` — and an empty list `[]` is
falsy in Python, so `or` treated "explicitly asked for nothing" identically to "didn't
specify anything." Fixed to check `is None` explicitly, with a `ValueError` for a genuinely
empty list. Caught by an adversarial test written specifically to probe this
(`test_empty_scenario_keys_raises_rather_than_silently_misbehaving`), which is now a
permanent regression test.

Everything else tested held up: LP constraint satisfaction (demand balance to 1e-4, capacity
caps, take-or-pay floor, supplier export caps, non-negativity), the graph's structural
integrity (no orphaned edges, no duplicate IDs, probabilities summing to 1.0), signal
extraction on all 7 headlines plus boundary severities, briefing generation on edge cases
(no headline, no supplier shifts, no incremental gap), and app-level stress paths (both λ
extremes, both Day-slider extremes, scenario-switch races, all four Assumptions sliders
pushed to both extremes at once).

**What this test pass explicitly does NOT cover** (from `TESTING.md`, unchanged): no visual
rendering verification (map/chart correctness was checked by reading the underlying data,
never by looking at pixels), no real LLM call has ever actually been exercised (no API key in
this environment — only the fallback paths are tested), no load/concurrency testing, no
hand-corrupted `network.json` testing.

### LLM contract + manual override + visual charts (most recent work)

- **Signal schema documented explicitly** in `signal_extractor.py`'s module docstring:
  `corridor`, `severity`, `probability`, `rationale`, plus a new field,
  `estimated_duration_days` — added because the Disruption Timeline's "days to fully
  re-contract" slider previously had no connection to how severe the triggering signal
  actually was. Explicitly documented what was considered and left OUT of the schema:
  `affected_suppliers` (a sanctions-style, supplier-specific disruption has nowhere to go,
  since every scenario in `scenario_engine.SCENARIOS` is corridor-based, not supplier-based —
  a real, named gap, not a silent omission).
- **`build_manual_signal()`** added as a third signal source alongside "llm" and
  "rule_based_fallback": a human sets corridor/severity/probability/duration/rationale
  directly via sliders in a new "Manual Signal Override" panel in `app.py`, bypassing text
  extraction entirely. Produces the exact same schema shape, so nothing downstream needs to
  know which of the three sources produced a given signal.
- **Bug found and fixed while wiring this in:** setting the Timeline's `n_days` slider's
  session-state value programmatically (from the signal's `estimated_duration_days`) in the
  same script run where that slider is also given a literal default `value=` argument
  triggered a Streamlit widget-policy warning ("created with a default value but also had its
  value set via Session State"). Fixed using the same session-state-first initialization
  pattern already used elsewhere in the app (check `if key not in st.session_state` before
  instantiating, then omit `value=` from the widget call).
- **Numeric tables replaced with charts** in three places: refinery utilization (was a
  5-column dataframe, now a color-coded horizontal bar — red under 60% served, amber 60-90%,
  green above 90%), supplier contract shift (was a 3-column dataframe, now a diverging bar
  chart, red=cut/green=grew), and per-refinery shortfall redistribution (was two numeric
  columns, now a paired bar chart). Raw numbers were not deleted — they're in a
  `st.expander("Raw numbers")` under each chart.
- Test suite grown from 81 to 95 tests to cover `build_manual_signal()`, the
  duration-from-severity bucketing, the manual-mode UI path (including toggling between
  headline and manual mode without crashing), and that a manual signal with the same
  corridor/severity as a headline-derived one maps to the same scenario.

---

## 4. Places most likely to have a real problem — a reviewer's shortlist

This section exists because "everything I built" isn't the same as "everything that's
right." In order of how likely each is to actually matter:

1. **The ~300 kbpd baseline topology gap** (`KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md` item under "residual
   supply gap") is a real, acknowledged incompleteness in the port/pipeline graph, not fully
   eliminated — only netted out of the reported headline numbers. If a reviewer wants to
   press on one thing, this is it.
2. **`alt_corridor`/`alt_transit_days` fields are dead data** — recorded in `network.json`
   but never consumed by any code path. Any reroute-cost/reroute-time behavior implied by the
   map or the data is not actually modeled.
3. **The rule-based signal extraction is a narrow, curated heuristic**, tuned specifically to
   this project's 7 headlines (e.g. treating "Iran" as a Hormuz-keyword). It has not been
   tested against any headline outside this set and would likely misclassify one.
4. **No real LLM call has ever been executed against this codebase.** Every "llm" code path
   (in `llm.py`, `signal_extractor.py`, `briefing_generator.py`) is written defensively but
   unverified in practice — `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` has never been set in any environment this
   was tested in.
5. **The optimizer's scenario probabilities (`scenario_engine.SCENARIOS`) are static planning
   assumptions**, not updated to reflect that a Hormuz-closure-like event is, as of the
   project's own "today," real and ongoing rather than a 5% tail risk. This tension is noted
   in an earlier conversation turn but not resolved in the code.
6. **Visual/rendering correctness for the map and charts has never been confirmed by looking
   at them** — every verification in this project reads the underlying data or catches
   Python exceptions; nothing has rendered a pixel and checked it.

---

## 5. Independent audit — findings and fixes

This shortlist (section 4 above) was handed to an independent reviewer, who ran the code —
not just read it — and found three HIGH-severity bugs that changed the demo's actual numbers,
plus five MEDIUM and eight LOW findings. The full audit is preserved verbatim in
`AUDIT_FINDINGS.md`. Every HIGH/MEDIUM claim was independently reproduced against the live
solved model before any fix was made (values matched the audit exactly). All fixes below have
landed; all 95 tests (82 core + 13 AppTest) still pass with zero regressions.

**H1 — domestic supply was stranded at ports.** `optimizer.py`'s `port_balance_rule` and
`scenario_engine.py`'s `refinery_supply_gap` both only counted `shipping_route` edges as port
inflow; the two `domestic_pipeline` edges that terminate at a port first (Mumbai offshore,
Rajasthan → Vadinar — ~450 kbpd, 80% of India's indigenous production) had flow=0.0 in every
scenario, no matter what. Fixed by counting `domestic_pipeline` inflow at ports in both places.
Effect: Mumbai BPCL's baseline shortfall (160.9 kbpd) dropped to 0; the baseline topology gap
fell from ~297 kbpd to **164.8 kbpd** (Panipat 132.5 + Kochi 31.3 + Bathinda 1.0 — see
`KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md`); baseline spot purchases fell from 257.0 to ~208.7 kbpd.

**H3 — domestic production had no cap in the optimizer.** `supplier_cap_rule` only capped
`shipping_route` contracts; domestic suppliers (via `domestic_pipeline`, in `OTHER_EDGES`) were
capped only by pipeline capacity, letting the solver draw more crude than a field actually
produces (Assam 108.5 used vs. 67.5 produced; Gujarat 153.9 vs. 45.0). Fixed with a new
`domestic_supplier_cap` constraint (flow ≤ real `flow_kbpd` production, per scenario). Landed
together with H1, since unstranding Mumbai/Rajasthan without this cap would have let the solver
overdraw them the same way.

**H2 — the optimizer drained the SPR on an undisrupted day.** Baseline (no disruption) SPR draw
was 304.5 kbpd — Vizag maxed at 140.0, Padur at 164.5 — because `SPR_DRAWDOWN_PREMIUM_USD_BBL`
($3/bbl) made SPR crude cheaper than several real suppliers, with no per-period depletion cost.
Fixed two ways: `spr_draw[e, "baseline"] == 0` is now a hard constraint (reserves are for
disruptions), and the premium was raised to $12/bbl so SPR prices above every routine barrel
even in disrupted scenarios. Baseline SPR draw is now 0.0 kbpd, confirmed.

**M2 — Haldia was a dead-end port.** `e_other_haldia` shipped 160 kbpd into `port_haldia`, which
had zero outgoing edges (Haldia refinery is actually fed via PHBPL from Paradip). Added
`e_haldia_port_to_ref` (160 kbpd, matching Haldia refinery's real ~160.7 kbpd capacity) so the
port has a real outlet.

**M3 — PHBPL's shared pipeline capacity was double-counted.** `e_paradip_haldia_ref` and
`e_paradip_barauni_ref` each independently carried the full 409.7 kbpd pipeline capacity, with
nothing enforcing that they share one physical pipe. Added a generic `shared_capacity_group`
mechanism (tag any `other_flow_edge` with a group id + cap) and a joint constraint in
`optimizer.py`; PHBPL's two edges are now capped at a combined 409.7 kbpd. Not binding in any
scenario today (max observed combined flow ~23 kbpd) but no longer silently wrong.

**M4 — port throughput caps didn't exist.** No code read a port node's own `capacity_kbpd`;
6 of 11 ports had inbound shipping capacity nominally exceeding their own stated throughput.
Added a `port_throughput` constraint capping total inflow (shipping + domestic) at each port's
real capacity. Confirmed non-binding at baseline for all 11 ports after the fix (largest
utilization: Sikka at 1,377/1,950 kbpd).

**M1 — the Brent-price headline maps to baseline, on purpose.** `h4_brent_100` has no corridor
keyword (it's a price consequence, not a chokepoint threat), so it correctly extracts to
`baseline`. Rather than silently patch this, it's now documented with a `note` field on the
headline and surfaced in the UI via `st.info()` when selected, framed as evidence the extractor
doesn't overreact to price headlines — a deliberate design choice, not a discovered surprise.

**M5 — the "Hormuz-routed capacity" KPI overstated exposure.** It summed raw `capacity_kbpd` of
Hormuz-tagged edges (2,800 kbpd, 57%) instead of weighting by `chokepoint_exposure` (2,380 kbpd,
48% — closer to the problem statement's own cited 40-45%). Fixed; the KPI is now labeled
"Hormuz exposure (weighted)".

**L1/L4/L6/L7 — polish and correctness debt.** Removed the stale "Coming Day 3+" sidebar note.
Added `coverage_fraction()` to `scenario_engine.py` and a UI warning when the SPR can only cover
part of a gap (e.g. hormuz_100: gap 678.5 kbpd vs. max SPR draw only covering ~70% of it) instead
of implying full coverage via "days of cover" alone. Threaded `network.json`'s file mtime into
the `st.cache_data` keys for `_run_comparison`/`_run_sensitivity`/`load_network` so a rebuilt
network file is never served from a stale cache. Added a warning banner when the Scenario picker
has changed since the last optimizer run, so an old briefing is never mistaken for describing the
current selection.

**What changed as a result, for the record:** baseline topology gap ~297 → 164.8 kbpd; baseline
SPR draw 304.5 → 0.0 kbpd; baseline spot purchases 257.0 → ~208.7 kbpd; Mumbai BPCL baseline
shortfall 160.9 → 0.0 kbpd. `KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md`'s residual-gap figure and `network.json`'s
own `meta.assumptions` text were both updated to match.

---

## 6. Second independent review — animated map, routing realism, and the briefing's structure

Between section 5 and this one, `animated_map.py` (a client-side deck.gl component with 60fps
flow-dot animation, a disruption pulse, and a timeline scrubber) and a restructured `app.py`
(a "Sense -> Simulate -> Prescribe" command-center layout) appeared in this project — built in a
separate session/window, not documented anywhere until now. This section covers the review of
that update plus a further round of fixes made directly in response to specific user feedback
after seeing it running.

**Bug — the animated map's basemap never rendered.** Screenshot evidence: pure black background,
only the deck.gl edges/dots/nodes visible, no landmasses at all ("the countries does not show").
Root cause: the map used `maplibre-gl` + a CARTO-hosted vector tile style
(`dark-matter-nolabels-gl-style`) as its basemap, and that live tile/style fetch was silently
failing inside Streamlit's `components.html` iframe. Fixed by dropping the live tile-server
dependency entirely: land is now a single static TopoJSON fetch (`world-atlas`, decoded
client-side with `topojson-client`) rendered as one `deck.GeoJsonLayer`, with `maplibre-gl`
removed. One GET instead of a raster/vector tile pipeline is a much smaller failure surface, and
if it does fail, it fails silently (dots/lines still render on black) rather than blocking
anything.

**Realism — routes were literal straight lines, cutting across landmass.** Nigeria/Angola/Russia
routes already bent through a real chokepoint waypoint (Cape of Good Hope, Gibraltar/Suez), but
USA Gulf Coast routes (`via_corridor=None` by design — this lane deliberately avoids both Hormuz
and Suez) had no bend at all, so the rendered line crossed the Atlantic and North Africa/Middle
East on screen. Added a render-only `EDGE_RENDER_WAYPOINTS` lookup (mirrors the existing
render-only `CORRIDOR_PATH_WAYPOINTS` pattern) bending USA routes around the Cape — consistent
with the ~23-24 day transit time already modeled for them, which is a Cape routing duration, not
a Suez one. Zero effect on `via_corridor`/`chokepoint_exposure`/the optimizer.

**Data honesty — the "Other (26 countries)" supplier node sat on top of Singapore.** An earlier
version anchored it there reasoning that it's a real trading hub and all its edges route via
Malacca anyway — but visually that reads as "this marker IS Singapore," misleading for a bucket
whose named constituents (Egypt, Colombia, Brazil, Qatar, Oman, +21 more) span the Gulf, Red Sea,
Atlantic, and Pacific. Moved to open water in the Andaman Sea (7.0, 95.0) — still adjacent to the
Malacca approach every edge in the bucket actually uses, no longer sitting on one specific real
country. `KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md` item 9 updated; the underlying routing approximation it already
disclosed is unchanged (this was a presentation fix, not a data fix).

**"Seamless" — every widget touch remounted the whole map.** Two concrete mitigations, both
verifiable without a live browser: (1) camera viewState now persists to `sessionStorage` across
Streamlit reruns (wrapped in try/catch — some iframe sandboxing blocks storage outright, in
which case it degrades to the old reset-every-time behavior, never worse); (2) the sidebar's node
and edge-type filters moved behind an `st.form` ("Apply filters" submit button) so toggling
checkboxes no longer triggers an immediate full rerun per click. The Assumptions tab's sliders
were deliberately NOT wrapped in a form, to avoid destabilizing `test_robustness_panel_extreme_
sliders_simultaneously`'s widget-index assumptions (`at.button[2]`, `at.slider[3..6]`) — a
narrower, lower-risk scope than a full rewrite to a bidirectional custom component, which is what
true zero-flash seamlessness would actually require.

**Real-world validation — checked, not assumed.** The "2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis" this build's
headlines are based on is not a hypothetical: live web search on 2026-07-21 confirms the strait
has been effectively closed to commercial shipping since 28 Feb 2026 and still is today. Checked
this model's own numbers directly against current reporting: Hormuz-routed import share (this
model: 48.2% exposure-weighted; reported: 40-52% across multiple sources) and capacity/flow lost
under full closure (this model: 48.2%; reported: "over 40%" of India's crude flows lost) both land
inside or very close to the real range; SPR inventory (5.33 MMT, ~9.5 days) matches independently
reported figures exactly (same underlying PIB source); and the model's own risk-averse
diversification-away-from-Hormuz direction matches India's real reported move to ~70% non-Hormuz
sourcing. Full writeup with citations: `KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md` item 8a. Surfaced in the app itself
via an `st.info()` callout when a Hormuz scenario is selected, plus a fuller side-by-side table in
the Data & methods tab.

**Briefing restructured to answer four specific questions.** The user asked, in effect: what's
the impact (plain words), how does it affect India, what should be done, what's the plan — not
just a cost delta buried in prose. `briefing_generator.py`'s template (and the LLM system prompt,
for when a real key is configured) now use exactly four labeled sections — Impact / Effect on
India / Recommended actions / Response plan — drawing on the same underlying facts as before plus
newly-added ones (SPR drawdown volume, spot-purchase volume, worst-hit refineries) so each section
has real numbers to answer with, not just the cost-comparison figures the old template had. Fixed
a real numbering bug in the process: the old draft hardcoded "1./2./3." literals, so skipping an
inapplicable action (e.g. no spot purchases needed) left a numbering gap, and a sub-list of
supplier gainers repeated "1." on every line instead of incrementing — both covered by new
regression tests.

**Layout — Sense and Simulate became tabs too.** Independent-review feedback: too much content
stacked on the main page above the original 4 tabs (sidebar, header, command bar, KPI strip, map,
refinery-detail expander — all before reaching a tab). Wrapped that content in two new tabs
(`Sense`, `Simulate`), for six total: Sense / Simulate / Prescription / Timeline / Assumptions &
robustness / Data & methods. Mechanically this only meant moving existing statements inside
`with tab_sense:` / `with tab_simulate:` blocks and re-indenting — Streamlit executes every tab's
body on every rerun regardless of which is visually active, so no cross-reference (session_state
or plain Python variable) broke, and the full 95+ test AppTest suite (which indexes widgets by
creation order) needed zero index changes.

**Verification for all of the above:** every fix in this section was verified by re-running the
full test suite after each change — 82 → 99 → 101 core tests (14 new tests added for
`animated_map.py`, 2 for the briefing restructure) and all 13 AppTest browser-level tests, with
one test fixed along the way (`test_extract_signal_twice_in_a_row_different_headlines` — its
markdown-block filter assumed the briefing template's own text repeated "Executive Briefing",
which the four-section restructure removed; the filter was broadened to check the whole rendered
page instead, which is the more correct check for what the test actually intends to verify).

## 7. Third independent review — six tabs rejected as unintuitive, rebuilt as three

Direct user feedback on the six-tab layout from Section 6: "the ui looks very unintuitive.. there
should be one tab which shows current system clearly and explains clearly too / then there should
be scenarios tab with clear information and not just random graphs.. then there should be the
news tab which also gives complete end to end information", followed by "each tab should also be
so intuitive that anybody who does not know anything about it is able to understand / if possible
use html and jss for frontend". This was a full front-end rebuild, not an incremental fix — the
six-tab Sense/Simulate/Prescription/Timeline/Assumptions/Data-and-methods layout is gone.

**New structure — exactly three tabs.**
- **Current system** — plain-English explanation of what the model is and does (no jargon), a row
  of baseline stat cards (imports, domestic production, refining capacity, emergency reserve,
  Hormuz dependence — each with a one-line plain-language meaning), the baseline (no-disruption)
  map, and two expanders ("How this system works", "Where these numbers come from") holding the
  methodology, sources, node/edge tables, and the real-world validation comparison table that used
  to occupy the whole Data & methods tab.
- **Scenarios** — pick any disruption from a plain dropdown; the optimizer auto-solves (cached,
  sub-second — no "Run optimizer" click needed anymore) and the tab leads with four plain-language
  narrative cards (Impact / Effect on India / What to do / The plan), THEN the map, THEN a
  "technical breakdown" expander holding everything from the old Prescription/Timeline/Assumptions
  tabs (KPI strip, the full technical briefing, cost/shift/refinery charts, and the robustness
  sliders) — present, not deleted, just no longer the first thing on screen.
- **News** — pick a real dated headline, click "See what this means for India", get the exact same
  plain-language-story → map → technical-breakdown experience inline, addressing the specific
  complaint that the old Sense tab only showed 5 summary metrics and made a judge switch tabs to
  see the actual impact.

**Plain-language translation layer, not just fewer tabs.** Added `briefing_generator.plain_language_story()`,
which takes the exact same `facts` dict the technical `_template_briefing()` uses (so it can never
drift from real optimizer output) and rewrites it in jargon-free sentences: "kbpd" becomes
"thousand barrels a day", "SPR" becomes "emergency oil reserve", cost deltas are framed as "this
costs about X% more than doing nothing special — think of it as an insurance premium" instead of a
raw CVaR-tinged percentage. The technical `_template_briefing()` text is not gone — it's one click
away in each tab's "technical breakdown" expander, for anyone who wants the underlying numbers.

**HTML/CSS card components, not default widget/matplotlib look.** Per "use html and jss for
frontend", the four narrative sections (and the Current-system stat row) are rendered as styled
HTML cards (`st.markdown(..., unsafe_allow_html=True)`, one small CSS block injected once at the
top of `app.py`) with color-coded left borders (red=Impact, orange=Effect, teal=Actions,
blue=Plan) rather than `st.metric` grids or a wall of text. This is deliberately NOT a full
React/JS rewrite of the whole app — Streamlit's own widgets (selectbox, radio, slider, button)
still drive state changes, since rebuilding that in raw JS would mean re-implementing session
state and reactivity from scratch for no real benefit. The one already-existing genuine JS/deck.gl
component (`animated_map.py`) is unchanged and reused by all three tabs via a single shared
`render_map()` helper.

**Consolidation, named explicitly:** the old Sense tab's dedicated "Manual Signal Override" panel
(raw corridor/severity/duration/probability sliders standing in for an LLM call) was removed as a
separate feature — the Scenarios tab's scenario picker already covers "test a disruption directly
without a headline," so keeping both was redundant complexity working against the "make this
simpler" ask. If fine-grained manual signal control (e.g. a severity value between two scenarios)
turns out to be missed, it's a small, isolated addition to bring back.

**Verification.** `app.py` was rebuilt from scratch rather than patched incrementally (the old
tab-body content was reorganized into three shared functions — `render_scenario_experience()`,
`render_technical_breakdown()`, `render_map()` — called by both the Scenarios and News tabs so the
two stay in lock-step rather than drifting). `tests/test_app_integration.py` was rewritten
end-to-end: widget indices were captured by actually running `AppTest` and printing every widget's
index and label (Streamlit's `AppTest` lists elements in DOM/tree order, not strict script
execution order — sidebar widgets end up interleaved with tab widgets in a way that isn't obvious
from reading the source), not assumed from the old six-tab file's indices. All 14 rewritten
AppTest cases pass, plus the full 101-test core suite (`test_briefing_generator.py`,
`test_animated_map.py`, `test_data_integrity.py`, `test_optimizer.py`, `test_scenario_engine.py`,
`test_signal_extractor.py`) — 115 tests total, zero regressions. Both the animated and static
(offline) map paths were exercised for all three tabs, including scenario switching, headline
switching, and extreme robustness-slider values.

## 8. Fourth independent review — visual polish, tabs restored, real ocean routing

Direct feedback on the three-tab redesign from Section 7: "well the scenario tab looks shit and
the other tabs does not look professional at all.. make them look professional and also add some
more important tabs which you removed.. instead of making information clustered.. use clickable
cards and hover text... make the dashboard have all the information that is required without
making it look bad." Two decisions were confirmed with the user before rebuilding (via
clarifying questions, since "add back tabs" and "keep it simple" pull in opposite directions):
keep Current system / Scenarios / News as the three main tabs, but add back a "Details & charts"
tab and a standalone "Data & methods" tab; go with a dark, data-dashboard visual style.

**Bug fix, reported separately mid-round:** "the stars to make the text bold is not working so it
looks weird with just stars." Root cause: `briefing_generator.plain_language_story()`'s strings
used Markdown `**word**` emphasis, but those strings are always embedded inside raw HTML `<div>`
cards via `st.markdown(html, unsafe_allow_html=True)` — Streamlit does not re-process Markdown
syntax found inside a raw HTML block, so the asterisks rendered as literal text instead of bold.
Fixed by switching every emphasized span in `plain_language_story()` to real `<strong>` tags.
Two regression tests added (`test_plain_language_story_uses_html_bold_not_markdown_asterisks`,
`..._baseline_has_no_markdown_asterisks`) that assert no returned string contains a literal `**`.

**Five tabs now: Current system / Scenarios / News / Details & charts / Data & methods.** The
last two are new homes for content that existed before the three-tab cut but had been folded into
a single expander — restored to full top-level tabs instead, per explicit user choice among three
offered options. `Details & charts` deliberately has its OWN scenario picker and risk-aversion
slider, independent of whatever is selected in Scenarios or News: Streamlit reruns every tab's
body on every interaction anywhere on the page, so there is no reliable way to detect "which tab
did the user actually just touch" — a self-contained tab is more predictable than one that quietly
mirrors invisible cross-tab state (and occasionally shows the wrong context because of it).
`Data & methods` holds sources, assumptions, node/edge tables, PPAC-verified figures, and the
real-world-validation comparison table, moved out of a Current-system expander into a proper home.

**Dark, data-dashboard visual theme.** Added `.streamlit/config.toml` with `base="dark"` and a
navy/teal palette (`backgroundColor #0b1220`, `secondaryBackgroundColor #111c2e`, `primaryColor
#2dd4bf`) — this themes every native widget (buttons, sliders, dataframes, tabs, pills)
consistently, which is far more robust than hand-styling individual widgets via CSS, since
Streamlit's internal DOM classes are emotion-hashed and not stable across sessions/versions. The
custom narrative/stat/hero cards got a matching dark CSS block (`.pt-card`, `.pt-hero`,
`.pt-stat`, `.pt-section-title`) with color-coded accent borders and `[title] {cursor: help;}` so
hover tooltips (native `title="..."` attributes on every card, explaining what each metric/section
actually means) are visually discoverable, not just present in the markup.

**"Clickable cards" — `st.pills`, not a hand-rolled HTML/JS grid.** The scenario picker (Scenarios
and Details & charts tabs) became `st.pills` with short icon-prefixed labels ("🟢 Normal day", "🔴
Hormuz — fully closed", etc.) — a real native Streamlit widget that already renders as a row of
clickable, hover-highlighted pill-buttons, fully driven by session state and fully addressable in
`AppTest` (`at.pills[i].set_value(...)`). Deliberately not a custom HTML/JS card grid wired back to
Python via query params or a bidirectional component: that would have meant re-implementing
click-to-state plumbing Streamlit already provides, for a widget `st.pills` already draws as cards.
The one place a genuine custom JS/deck.gl component earns its keep — `animated_map.py` — is
unchanged and still shared by every tab via `render_map()`.

**Verification.** `tests/test_app_integration.py` was rewritten again for the 5-tab widget layout
(indices re-captured by actually running `AppTest`, not assumed) — 17 tests, including a dedicated
regression test for the bold-text bug and one confirming the Details tab's scenario picker is
independent of the Scenarios tab's. Full suite after this round: 107 non-AppTest tests +17 AppTest
= 124, zero regressions.

**Real ocean routing, from a direct follow-up question:** "is there a way to make routes on the
map as actual routes instead of point A and point B being joined together with straight line using
actual route data over the ocean?" Added the `searoute` package (Apache-2.0, MIT-licensed
dependency-wise, ships its own maritime traffic-lane graph — no network call needed, so the
existing offline/static-map guarantee still holds) and a new `_real_sea_path()` helper in
`app.py`, cached via `st.cache_data`. Every one of the network's 21 real shipping routes resolves
through it cleanly (verified in `tests/test_maritime_routing.py`); the old
`CORRIDOR_PATH_WAYPOINTS`/`EDGE_RENDER_WAYPOINTS` manual-bend logic is kept only as a fallback for
a pair `searoute` can't resolve (none today), since real sea routes already thread through Suez,
the Cape of Good Hope, or Hormuz/Malacca correctly on their own — the old single-manual-waypoint
approach was always an approximation standing in for exactly this. `test_maritime_routing.py`
(4 new tests) checks every shipping edge resolves without hitting the fallback, that long-haul
routes (USA, Russia, Nigeria, Angola to India) actually have multiple waypoints rather than
degenerating back to a straight line, that snapped endpoints stay close to the real node
coordinates, and that a known short-haul corridor (Iraq → Sikka via Hormuz) produces a plausible
distance rather than a nonsensical detour.

## 9. Splitting the pooled "Other" supplier node into 25 named countries

Direct follow-up request: "can we separate each country in the nodes that combine 26 countries
together so the network looks full?" The pooled "Other" node (9.08% of imports, 26 countries)
was accurate in total volume but made the map look emptier than reality and was already flagged
as a real limitation (KNOWN_LIMITATIONS.md item 9 -- it mixed Gulf/Red-Sea-side countries like
Egypt/Oman/Qatar in with a bucket routed entirely via Malacca, which was geographically wrong for
that slice of it).

**Source data, not invented.** The raw DGCI&S export behind this project's supplier shares
(`data/dgcis_freeuser_1784552528488_1.xls`) already lists every individual country by name and
tonnage -- the aggregation into "Other" was a modeling choice on top of real per-country data that
was sitting in the file the whole time. Re-extracted it directly: 34 countries reported for
`PETROLEUM: CRUDE` (the file also has a separate `PETROLEUM PRODUCTS` block, correctly excluded --
refined products, not crude); Hungary reported exactly 0 tonnes and is the one country genuinely
omitted; the remaining 25 sum to 9.07% of the crude total, matching "Other"'s old 9.08% share
almost exactly (the 0.01pt gap is rounding in the original 4-decimal share, not a data error).

**Each of the 25 got real modeling, not just a name.** Every one has its own supplier node with a
real principal export terminal (Ras Laffan for Qatar, Ceyhan for Turkey, Sidi Kerir for Egypt,
Covenas for Colombia, etc. -- see `build_network.py` SUPPLIER_META comments), a corridor/
chokepoint-exposure assignment based on that terminal's actual geography (Gulf-inside -> Hormuz at
high exposure; Mediterranean/Red-Sea-adjacent -> Suez; West/Central-African Atlantic -> the Cape
bypass at zero exposure, same treatment as Nigeria/Angola; Americas -> no named chokepoint, same as
USA; Southeast Asia -> Malacca), and a shipping edge to a specific Indian port sized at roughly
1.6x its real 2025 flow (same margin convention as every other supplier edge in the network).

**A real bug caught by the existing test suite, not a hypothetical:** deleting the two original
pooled edges into `port_haldia` left that port with zero shipping-route inflow (it only had the
one), which doesn't make the optimizer report infeasible -- Pyomo's constraint builder crashes
outright (`InvalidConstraintError: trivial Boolean`) because summing over zero edges collapses a
relational expression into a plain Python `True`. Caught immediately by `test_optimizer.py`'s
existing full-suite run; fixed by landing South Korea's new edge at Haldia instead of Chennai. A
new regression test (`test_every_port_has_at_least_one_shipping_route_inflow`) locks this in going
forward so a future edit can't silently reintroduce it.

**A second bug, caught by a new test, not shipped silently:** Canada's crude export terminal was
initially placed on the Pacific coast (Vancouver) -- a Canada-to-India sea route crossing the
Pacific means crossing the antimeridian, which `searoute` represents as a continuously decreasing
longitude (e.g. -279.6 degrees instead of 80.4 degrees E) rather than wrapping back into
[-180, 180]. A naive Euclidean distance check in the new `test_maritime_routing.py` test flagged
this as looking like a snapping failure; investigating showed it's mathematically the same point,
just an unwrapped longitude -- legitimate for how deck.gl draws antimeridian-crossing paths, but an
unnecessary edge case for one 0.05%-share country. Moved Canada's terminal to the Atlantic coast
(Saint John, NB) instead, sidestepping the wraparound entirely; the distance-check test itself was
also fixed to wrap longitude differences properly, since the underlying routing library's behavior
was correct all along.

**Result:** 47 -> 71 nodes, 45 -> 65 edges. Total import/domestic/refinery-capacity totals are
unchanged (4936.2 / 562.3 / 5004.4 kbpd) -- this only changed how the same total is broken down by
supplier, not any headline number the app reports. `optimizer.py`'s per-supplier price-differential
dict deliberately has no new entries for the 25 countries (same as "Other" before them -- they
default to the plain India Basket price, since none of them have a documented market discount/
premium the way the top-8 do).

**Verification.** Two new tests in `test_data_integrity.py`
(`test_every_port_has_at_least_one_shipping_route_inflow`,
`test_other_aggregate_supplier_is_gone_and_replaced_by_named_countries`) plus the full existing
suite: 107 non-AppTest tests + 17 AppTest tests, all passing after the network regeneration --
including every scenario, every headline, and both the animated and static map paths, none of
which needed code changes to handle 24 more supplier nodes (NODE_COLORS/NODE_RADIUS key off node
*type*, not individual node id, so new suppliers inherit the existing "supplier" red styling
automatically).
